Lithuania is officially known as the Republic of Lithuania.
Lithuania is located in Europe, bordered by several countries including Latvia, Belarus, Poland, and Russia.
The country has an area of 65,300 km2 (25,200 sq mi) and an estimated population of approximately 2.9 million in 2025.
The capital and largest city of Lithuania is Vilnius, with other major cities being Kaunas and Klaipėda.
Lithuanians are the titular nation and form the majority of the population, speaking the Lithuanian language.
The national anthem of Lithuania is called the 'National Hymn.'
In terms of economic indicators, Lithuania's GDP (PPP) is estimated to be substantial, with a per capita income of $55,995 in 2025 (PPP).
Lithuania is in the time zone of +2 and observes a summer time zone of +3.
For millennia, the southeastern shores of the Baltic Sea were inhabited by various groups.
In the 1230s, Lithuanian lands were united for the first time by leaders who formed the monarchy on 6 July 1253.
Subsequent expansion and consolidation resulted in the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, which by the 14th century was the largest country in Europe.
In 1386, the Grand Duchy entered into a union with the Kingdom of Poland.
The two realms were united into the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth in 1569, forming one of the largest and most prosperous states in Europe.
The Commonwealth lasted more than two centuries, until neighbouring countries gradually partitioned it between 1772 and 1795, with the Russian Empire annexing most of Lithuania's territory.
Towards the end of World War I, Lithuania declared independence in 1918, founding the modern Republic of Lithuania.
Lithuania was occupied in 1940, then reoccupied in 1944, during the Soviet occupations of the Baltic states.
The struggle against Soviet occupation lasted until the early 1950s.
On 11 March 1990, Lithuania became the first Soviet republic to declare independence from the Soviet Union.
Lithuania is a republic with a parliamentary democracy and a mixed economy.
Lithuania is a member of various international organizations and participates in regional cooperation formats.
The spelling of 'Lithuania' was standardized later than the original form 'Lituania' influenced by Latin.
The name 'Lietuva' is the original form of Lithuania and dates back to a story recorded on 9 March 1009.
The name Lietuva may derive from a small river and has been proposed to relate to a warrior social group called 'leičiai' in historical contexts.
The history of Lithuania dates back to settlements founded about 10,000 years ago.
The first settlers in Lithuania were traveling hunters in the 10th millennium BC.
Inhabitants engaged in hunting, gathering, and fishing with the climate warming by the 8th millennium BC.
The arrival of the Baltic tribes in the 3rd – 2nd millennium BC led to the formation of new cultural mixes with local populations.
Baltic tribes maintained trade contacts but did not have close political relations with outsiders.
From the 9th to the 11th centuries, coastal Balts faced raids, affecting their cultural development.
Lithuania comprised culturally different regions with distinct burial practices during early medieval times.
Lithuania's remote location contributed to its unique linguistic, cultural, and religious identity.
Traditional elements were preserved long, with cremation practices before the conversion to Christianity.
The first written record of Lithuania's name dates back to 1009 AD.
In the middle of the 13th century, Mindaugas united various tribes and founded the State of Lithuania.
Mindaugas was crowned as the first Catholic king of Lithuania in 1253.
Mindaugas incorporated territories weakened by external pressures into Lithuania.
After Mindaugas' assassination in 1263, Lithuania faced external threats again.
Traidenis reunified Lithuanian lands and achieved successes against the Crusaders during his reign from 1269 to 1282.
The late 13th century marked the beginning of the dynasty's rule in Lithuania, which consolidated its territories.
By the first half of the 14th century, Lithuania's territory expanded to approximately 650,000 km2, making it the largest country in Europe by the end of the 14th century.
In 1385, Lithuania formed a significant union with Poland through the Union of Krewo.
The Gediminids dynasty ruled over Lithuania and Poland, as well as territories including Hungary and others in late 14th to 15th centuries.
Lithuania achieved a decisive victory against German attacks in the Battle of Grunwald in 1410 and concluded the Treaty of Thorn in 1422.
In the 15th century, the strengthened Grand Duchy renewed its control over Lithuanian territories amid pressures from various monarchs.
The Union of Lublin in 1569 created a new Commonwealth with a joint monarch, although Lithuania retained its separate state identity.
Post-Union, Lithuania and Poland experienced military successes during the Livonian War.
In 1588, the rights of Lithuania and Poland within the Commonwealth were confirmed, ensuring equality and enhancing the autonomy of Lithuanian nobility.
The mid-17th century was marked by disastrous military losses during the Deluge, leading to the annexation of much of Lithuania by the Tsardom of Russia.
Lithuania unilaterally separated from Poland in 1655 and declared the Swedish King as Grand Duke.
By 1657, Lithuania rejoined the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth after a revolt against the Swedes, recapturing Vilnius in 1661.
The Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth was dissolved in 1795 by three neighboring countries after a failed uprising in 1794.
Lithuania's territory was annexed following the dissolution.
After annexation, new administrative policies were implemented in Lithuania under foreign rule.
In 1812, Napoleon established a puppet government in Lithuania, which ended after his defeat and the reinstatement of Russian rule.
The November Uprising (1830-1831) was an attempt by Lithuanians and Poles to restore their statehood, but it was unsuccessful due to Russian victory.
Stricter Russification measures were enforced following the failed uprisings, including a ban on the Lithuanian language in government institutions and the closure of institutions.
Historical theories were propagated suggesting Lithuania was a 'Western Russian' state since its establishment.
Lithuanians attempted to restore statehood again during the January Uprising (1863-1864), leading to stronger Russification policies.
S. Daukantas promoted pre-Commonwealth traditions and wrote a history of Lithuania in Lithuanian, which was not published at the time.
A colleague of S. Daukantas, Narbutt, wrote a history of the Lithuanian Nation and highlighted the relationship between Lithuanian and Sanskrit.
The Lithuanians resisted Russification through secret Lithuanian publishing and homeschooling.
An extensive network inspired by Lithuanian history, language, and culture laid the foundations for the reestablishment of an independent Lithuania.
A significant meeting was held in 1905 where resolutions were adopted demanding wide autonomy for Lithuania.
In 1907, Lithuanians organized a resolution for the restoration of sovereignty and military alliance with Germany.
The short-lived 'Kingdom of Lithuania' was proclaimed in 1918 but was superseded by the restoration of independence on February 16, 1918.
Lithuanians defended statehood during conflicts with other nations from 1918 to 1920.
Plans for Lithuanian statehood clashed with Russian expansionist designs in the region.
Polish forces captured Vilnius in 1920, establishing control which was later incorporated into Poland.
In 1923, a movement unified Memel with Lithuania.
A regime replaced the democratically elected government in Lithuania, which led to significant political changes.
In the late 1930s, Lithuania accepted ultimatums and transferred the Klaipėda Region to Germany.
Following WWII, Lithuania experienced Soviet occupation, leading to resistance movements attempting to restore independence.
In 1941, Lithuania faced a brief attempt at independence before being reoccupied by the Soviets.
The Lithuanian resistance was brutally suppressed in the 1950s, with many leaders executed.
Since the late 1980s, a movement emerged seeking the restoration of independent Lithuania, culminating in the 1989 Congress.
Lithuania announced the restoration of independence on 11 March 1990, becoming the first Soviet-occupied state to do so.
The declaration of independence inspired other Soviet states and influenced future democratic movements.
The Soviet response included a blockade of Lithuania starting on 20 April 1990, leading to shortages of essential goods.
The blockade lasted for 74 days, during which Lithuania maintained its declaration of independence.
In January 1991, tensions peaked with a coup attempt that led to casualties as citizens defended independence.
14 people were killed and hundreds were injured during the coup efforts by Soviet forces in January 1991.
On 31 July 1991, Soviet paramilitaries killed 7 Lithuanian border guards on the Belarusian border.
Lithuania was admitted to the United Nations on 17 September 1991.
A referendum on 25 October 1992 led to the adoption of the current Lithuanian constitution.
The first president after the restoration of independence was elected on 14 February 1993.
The last units of the former Soviet Army left Lithuania on 31 August 1993.
Lithuania joined the World Trade Organization (WTO) on 31 May 2001 and became a member of NATO in March 2004.
Lithuania adopted the Euro as its currency on 1 January 2015.
Lithuania participated in NATO consultations on security in response to the 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine.
The NATO summit was held in Vilnius on 11–12 July 2023.
Lithuania is located in the Baltic region and covers an area of 65,300 km2 (25,200 sq mi).
Lithuania's geographical coordinates lie between specific latitudes and longitudes, with a sandy coastline of approximately 99 kilometers.
38 kilometers (24 mi) of Lithuania's coastline face the open Baltic Sea, less than other Baltic states, the remainder is sheltered by the Curonian sand peninsula.
The country's major lagoon is the Curonian Lagoon, which is at the mouth of the Neman River.
Lithuania lies at the edge of the East European Plain, and its terrain was shaped by glacial activity, featuring lowlands and highlands.
The highest point in Lithuania reaches 294 meters (965 ft).
Mixed forests cover over 33% of Lithuania's land, with lakes being significant geographic features, including the largest, deepest, and longest lakes in the country.
Jean-George Affholder identified the geographical center of Europe to be in Lithuania, located 26 kilometers (16 mi) north of Vilnius after a boundary re-estimation in 1989.
Lithuania has a temperate climate with continental and maritime influences, classified as Dfb under the Köppen climate classification.
Average temperatures range from −6 °C (21 °F) in January to 17 °C (63 °F) in July in Vilnius; coastal averages are also provided.
Summer temperatures can reach as high as 30 or 35 °C (86 or 95 °F), while winters can see temperatures drop below −20 °C (−4 °F) almost every winter.
Average annual precipitation: 800 mm on the coast, 900 mm in the Samogitia highlands, and 600 mm in the eastern part of Lithuania.
Snow occurs annually from October to April, with occasional sleet in September or May.
Growing season lasts 202 days in the western part and 169 days in the eastern part of Lithuania.
Severe storms are rare in eastern Lithuania but common in coastal areas.
Temperature records in the Baltic area cover about 250 years, showing warm periods in the late 18th century and a cooler 19th century.
Early 20th-century warming peaked in the 1930s, followed by a cooling until the 1960s and a persistent warming trend since then.
Lithuania faced a drought in 2002 that caused forest and bog fires.
The Environmental Protection Act was adopted in 1992, establishing rights and obligations for biodiversity and ecological preservation.
Lithuania committed to reducing greenhouse gas emissions by at least 20% from 1990 levels by 2020 and by at least 40% by 2030, alongside EU members.
By 2020, Lithuania aimed for at least 20% (27% by 2030) of energy consumption to come from renewable sources.
In 2016, Lithuania improved waste collection efficiency, achieving 92% of packaging recycling in 2017.
Lithuania's landscape features blooming meadows, dense forests, and fertile fields with thousands of lakes and numerous rivers.
Lithuania is drained by the longest rivers and has two distinct terrestrial ecoregions.
Forests are a crucial natural resource in Lithuania, occupying one-third of the country's territory.
Timber-related industrial production contributes nearly 11% to Lithuania's industrial production.
Lithuania has 5 state-protected natural heritage objects.
The country's ecosystems include natural and semi-natural (forests, wetlands, meadows) and anthropogenic (agrarian, urban) ecosystems.
Forests are particularly significant in Lithuania, covering 33% of its territory.
Wetlands have suffered significant loss, with 70% lost due to drainage and peat extraction between 1960 and 1980.
Changes in wetland plant communities have resulted in the replacement of moss and grass with trees and shrubs.
There are 29,000 rivers in Lithuania, totaling 64,000 km in length, with the basin covering 74% of the country.
Construction of dams has led to the disappearance of approximately 70% of potential fish spawning sites.
54% of Lithuania's territory is agricultural land, with 70% of that being arable land.
Around 400,000 ha of agricultural land is not actively farmed, creating an ecological niche for invasive plant species.
Habitat deterioration is occurring in productive agricultural regions as crop areas expand.
Many plant species, fungi, and lichens are included in a conservation list in Lithuania, representing a significant proportion of known species.
Wildlife populations have rebounded due to restricted hunting and urbanization, leading to forest replanting.
Lithuania has approximately 250,000 larger wild animals, averaging 5 per square kilometer.
The most common large wild animal is the elk, with 120,000 individuals.
Other ungulates include deer species and wild boar.
Predators, particularly wolves, are common, with approximately 27,000 present in Lithuania.
Mythical animals like the lynx are less common, with only around 800 in the country.
Rare species like the European bison number about 200, not counting other large animals that may be found in Lithuanian forests.
Lithuania restored its independence on 11 March 1990.
Lithuania held its first independent general elections on 25 October 1992.
The constitution of Lithuania was a topic of intense debate, especially regarding the role of the president.
A referendum regarding the constitution was held on 23 May 1992, with 41% voter support for the restoration of presidential powers.
The president of Lithuania is directly elected for a five-year term and can serve a maximum of two terms.
The president oversees foreign affairs and national security and serves as the commander of the military.
The president appoints the prime minister, cabinet members, and judges for all courts except the Constitutional Court.
The current head of state was re-elected in 2024, winning more than 74% of the run-off votes.
Judges of the Constitutional Court serve nine-year terms and are appointed by the Seimas based on nominations from the president and other officials.
The Lithuanian parliament, called Seimas, has 141 members, elected to four-year terms with a mix of single-member constituencies and national votes.
A party must receive at least 5% of the national vote to gain representation in the Seimas.
Lithuania was one of the first countries in the world to allow women to vote, granting this right in 1919.
Lithuania has a fragmented multi-party system with common coalition governments.
Ordinary elections occur on the second Sunday of October every four years.
Candidates for parliament must be at least 21 years old, eligible citizens, and must reside permanently in Lithuania.
Individuals serving prison sentences 65 days before an election, judges, military service members, and certain officials cannot stand for election.
The political party that gained 52 of 141 seats in parliament in the recent election is unspecified.
In November 2024, a coalition agreement led to a new prime minister from the Social Democrats.
The President is the head of state, elected for a five-year term with the majority vote, and can serve a maximum of two terms.
Candidates for president must be at least 40 years old and have resided in Lithuania for at least three years.
Each municipality in Lithuania is governed by a mayor and a municipal council member.
Municipal council members are elected every four years and the number varies based on the municipality's population.
Direct mayoral elections have been in place since 2015, with the mayor elected by the majority of the municipality's residents.
1,498 municipal council members were elected in 2023, with one political party winning 358 municipal council seats and 17 mayoral positions.
As of 2024, Lithuania has 11 allocated seats.
Ordinary elections in Lithuania are held on Sundays, coinciding with other EU countries' election dates.
Citizens of Lithuania and EU citizens permanently residing in Lithuania who are at least 18 years old can vote in elections.
Candidates for elections must be at least 21 years old and either be citizens of Lithuania or EU citizens residing in Lithuania.
Candidates cannot run for election in more than one country.
Individuals serving a court-imposed sentence of 65 days before the election are ineligible to vote or run for office.
Judges, military service personnel, and officials of statutory institutions are not permitted to stand for election.
In the 1934–1935 elections, certain individuals were sentenced to imprisonment.
After regaining independence in 1990, modified Soviet legal codes were in place for about ten years.
The current legal framework of Lithuania was adopted on 25 October 1992.
In 2001, a significant legal code was enacted, succeeded by new codes in 2003.
Lithuania's legal approach emphasizes formality and rationalization rather than practicality and informality.
Lithuanian legal system has been integral since 1 May 2004.
Lithuania experienced a difficult crime situation after breaking away from the Soviet Union.
Lithuanian law enforcement agencies have fought crime, leading to a decline in crime rates.
Local Lietuvos policija is primarily responsible for law enforcement in Lithuania.

Key law enforcement agencies include Lietuvos policijos antiteroristinių operacijų rinktinė Aras, Lietuvos kriminalinės policijos biuras, Lietuvos policijos kriminalistinių tyrimų centras, and Lietuvos kelių policijos tarnyba.
In 2017, 63,846 crimes were registered in Lithuania, with significant cases of theft and serious crimes declining.
129 homicides or attempted homicides were reported in 2017, which is a decrease from previous years.
Electronic data and information technology crime cases have increased.
29% of Lithuanians felt corruption affected their daily lives, with 95% considering it widespread.
Corruption levels in Lithuania have reportedly been decreasing over the past decade according to Transparency International.
Lithuania has the highest number of prison inmates in the EU due to high repression levels and lack of trust in the justice system.
The system of administrative division in Lithuania was established in 1994 and modified in 2000 to align with EU requirements.
Counties (apskritis) in Lithuania are subdivided into municipalities (savivaldybė), which are further divided into 546 elderships (seniūnija).
Lithuania has 5 distinct administrative regions recognized by the state.
Municipalities are the primary administrative units in Lithuania after the dissolution of the County governor system in 2010.
Some municipalities are historically referred to as 'district municipalities' while others are 'city municipalities.'
Each municipality has its own elected government, with council elections held every four years.
Mayors in Lithuania have been directly elected since 2015, prior to which they were appointed by the council.
Elderships are the smallest administrative units in Lithuania, responsible for providing local public services such as vital registrations and welfare distribution.
Elderships do not have a significant role in national politics and some citizens feel they lack power and recognition.
Lithuania became a member of the United Nations on 18 September 1991.
Lithuania is a signatory to various international agreements and organizations.
Lithuania is a member of NATO and its adjunct North Atlantic Coordinating Council.
Lithuania gained EU membership on 31 May 2001 and joined the Eurozone on 5 July 2018.
Lithuania has diplomatic relations with 149 countries.
In 2011, Lithuania hosted the Ministerial Council Meeting.
Lithuania assumed the role of the Presidency of the Council of the EU in the second half of 2013.
Lithuania is active in regional cooperation among northern European countries.
Lithuania cooperates with Nordic and Baltic countries through the NB6 format.
CBSS (Council of the Baltic Sea States) was established in 1992 as a regional political forum.
The main aim of CBSS is to promote integration and strengthen contacts between countries in the region.
CBSS member states include various countries and the European Commission, with observer states as well.
Lithuania is involved in the NORDPLUS programme focused on education.
The Baltic Development Forum (BDF) is an independent organization uniting various entities in the Baltic Sea region.
The BDF held its 12th summit in Vilnius in 2010.
Historical Polish support for Lithuanian independence is noted, despite criticisms over treatment of the Polish minority.
Lithuania maintains strong relations with Georgia and supports its EU and NATO aspirations.
In 2008, Lithuania's president visited Tbilisi to show support during the Georgian conflict with Russia.
Lithuania collected financial support for Georgian war victims during the 2008 conflict.
From 2004 to 2009, Lithuania served within a NATO-led Commission.
Lithuania was elected to the UN Security Council in 2013, being the first Baltic country to do so.
During its UN Security Council membership, Lithuania condemned various actions of Russia and supported Ukraine.
President Dalia Grybauskaitė has made statements comparing Russian leadership unfavorably and criticizing Russian actions.
In 2018, Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia received recognition for their democratic development and contributions to peace.
In 2019, Lithuania condemned a specific unnamed action.
In December 2021, Lithuania reported a diplomatic dispute with China over imports and faced a halt in trade with China.
In 2023, there was an increase in Chinese intelligence activities against Lithuania, including cyberespionage.
The 2023 event was held in the Lithuanian capital.
The Lithuanian Armed Forces consist of unified armed forces, including the Logistics Command, Training and Doctrine Command, and Military Police.
The Lithuanian Armed Forces have approximately 20,000 active personnel which may be supported by reserve forces.
Compulsory military service ended in 2008 but was reintroduced in 2015.
Lithuanian soldiers currently participate in international operations and EU training missions, specifically training Ukrainian soldiers.
Lithuania became a full member of NATO in March 2004, with NATO members deployed there.
Lithuania was part of ISAF in Afghanistan beginning in summer of 2005.
The Provincial Reconstruction Team (PRT) in Afghanistan included personnel from various countries.
Since joining international operations in 1994, Lithuania has lost two soldiers in Afghanistan due to enemy actions.
The Lithuanian National Defence Policy aims to preserve the independence and sovereignty of the state and protect territorial integrity.
The Ministry of National Defence of Lithuania oversees combat forces and intelligence operations.
The State Border Guard Service is responsible for border protection and customs duties, falling under the Ministry's supervision.
A special security department is responsible for VIP protection and related tasks.
In 2015, a new paramilitary organization was established as a civilian self-defense institution.
In 2020, Lithuania allocated more of its budget to national defense.
Lithuania lagged behind NATO allies in defense spending for a long time, especially after 2008.
Lithuania began to rapidly increase defense funding, exceeding the NATO guideline of 2% in 2019.
Lithuania's president called for more NATO troops on April 22, 2022, advocating for increased deployment on Europe's eastern flank.
Lithuania has an open economy classified as a high-income economy by the World Bank.
As of 2017, major sectors of Lithuania's economy include services (67% of GDP), industry (29%), and agriculture (3%).
Lithuania joined NATO and the EU in 2004, the Eurozone in 2015, and the OECD in 2018.
The euro became the national currency on January 1, 2015, replacing the Lithuanian litas.
Agricultural products and food account for 18% of Lithuania's exports, with other major sectors being chemical products, machinery, and mineral products.
Over half of Lithuania's exports go to seven countries, with Russia being the largest at 14% and Latvia at 10%.
Exports were 81% of GDP in 2017.
GDP growth peaked at 11% in 2007 but contracted by 15% in 2009 due to a financial crisis.
Unemployment reached significant levels in 2010, but growth has been slower since.
Public debt ratio in 2016 was 40% of GDP, up from 15% in 2008.
Over 95% of FDI comes from EU countries.
Sweden is the largest investor in Lithuania with 20% - 30% of FDI.
FDI into Lithuania spiked in 2017 with the highest recorded number of greenfield investment projects.
The US was the leading source of FDI in 2017, with 25% of the total.
Germany and the UK each represented 11% of total project numbers in 2017.
Exports in 2017 showed rapid growth of 17%, the fastest in the Baltic countries and across Europe.
Between 2004 and 2016, one out of five Lithuanians emigrated primarily due to insufficient income.
Long-term emigration and economic growth have caused a labor market shortage.
Unemployment in Lithuania was 8% in 2017.
Lithuanian wealth per adult was $32,000 in 2022; total national wealth was $147 billion.
The average monthly gross salary in Lithuania as of 2023 Q2 was €2,000.
Lithuania has a low personal income and corporate tax rate of 15%, one of the lowest in the EU.
Corporate tax rates are 15% and 5% for small businesses in Lithuania.
Production in Lithuania was growing, reaching €2 billion in 2016.
In 2017, 35 companies set up operations in Lithuania due to simplified government procedures.
Lithuania issued a total of 39 e-money licenses, second in the EU to the UK.
Europe's first international Blockchain Centre opened in Vilnius in 2018.
Since 2021, Lithuania has issued hundreds of licenses for cryptocurrency operations, becoming a leader in the EU.
Agriculture is mentioned as a sector.
Agriculture in Lithuania dates back to 3,000 to 1,000.
Agriculture has been a key occupation in Lithuania for centuries.
Lithuania joined the European Union in 2004, marking a new era for agriculture.
The EU mandates high standards for food safety and purity.
Lithuania's parliament adopted the Law on Product Safety in 1999 and the Law on Food in 2000.
Agricultural market reforms are based on the Law on Product Safety and the Law on Food.
In 2016, agricultural production in Lithuania was € billion.
The largest agricultural production type was unspecified at 5710 tons, followed by others including unspecified types at 934 tons, 393 tons, and 340 tons.
In 2016, Lithuania exported €4,385 million worth of agricultural products, with €3,165 million being of Lithuanian origin.
Agricultural and food products made up 19% of all Lithuanian exports.
Organic farming is increasing in popularity in Lithuania, with certification granted by Ekoagros.
In 2016, there were 2539 organic farms occupying 225,542 hectares, with 43% in cereals, 31% in perennial grasses, 14% in leguminous crops, and 12% in others.
A Lithuanian nobleman and expert popularized the concept of a specific scientific field (not specified).
The University of Vilnius, founded in 1579, helped foster a scientific community in Lithuania.
Prominent scientists and thinkers have been associated with the University of Vilnius.
A 17th century artillery expert published a manual, *Artis Magnae Artilleriae*, influential in military and civil rocket technology.
Botanist Tadas Blinda created the first guide to Lithuanian flora and a Latin-Lithuanian dictionary of plant names.
German scientist Karl Friedrich von Ramm proposed an unspecified scientific concept and worked on improving the well-being of peasants.
The world wars of the 20th century negatively impacted Lithuanian science and academia.
Lithuanian scholars achieved success abroad in various fields including philosophy, law, aviation, management, archaeology, primatology, and linguistics.
Jurgis Baltrušaitis is a notable Lithuanian mathematician and long-term rector, known for the Theorem of Kubilius and resisting Russification in academia.
Šviesos konversija (Light Conversion) is a leading Lithuanian company with a laser system that holds an 80% market share globally.
Applications of the laser system include DNA research, ophthalmological surgeries, and nanotechnology.
The Laser Research Center created a powerful femtosecond laser, among the most powerful in the world.
A. Ragauskas developed non-invasive devices for measuring intracranial pressure and blood flow.
Kęstutis Pyragas is known for his method of delayed feedback control in scientific research.
A notable laureate is recognized for discoveries related to CRISPR technology.
Lithuania has launched three satellites: a specific name is needed for each.
Lithuanian academic and research institutions collaborate with major research organizations in Europe.
In 2018, Lithuania became an Associated Member State with a notable research organization.
CERN incubators are set to be established in Vilnius and Kaunas.
The Life Sciences Center and Center For Physical Sciences and Technology are hotspots for advanced research in Lithuania.
As of 2016, Lithuania's biotech and life science sector experienced a 22% growth over the past five years.
There are 16 academic institutions and 15 R&D centers in Lithuania's life science and biotech sector.
The Valley development programme launched in 2008 to enhance scientific research infrastructure and promote collaboration between business and science.
Five R&D Valleys were established focusing on maritime technologies, agro/bioenergy/forestry, laser/light/semiconductors, biotechnology/medicine, and sustainable chemistry/pharmacy.
The Lithuanian Innovation Center was created to support innovation and research institutions.
Lithuania ranks 15th among EU countries.
Lithuania was ranked 35th in tourism in 2024.
Statistics from 2023 showed millions of tourists visited Lithuania.
The largest number of tourists came from specific countries such as Poland, Germany, and others.
Domestic tourism is increasing with up to 1000 places of attraction in Lithuania.
Popular cities and seaside resorts attract most tourists.
Hot air ballooning is a popular activity, especially in Vilnius and Trakai.
Bicycle tourism is growing, especially along the Lithuanian Seaside Cycle Route.
Total bicycle track length in Lithuania amounts to 3769 km.
Tourism's contribution to GDP was forecasted to rise to € billion by 2027, but decreased in 2023.
Lithuania has a well-developed communications infrastructure with a large number of SIM cards.
Lithuania's LTE (4G) mobile network covers 97% of its territory.
Lithuania was ranked among the top countries in mobile and fixed broadband speeds in 2017.
Lithuania ranked 17th in the UN's e-participation index in 2016.
There are four TIER III data centers in Lithuania, and it ranks 44th globally in data center density.
The RAIN project aimed to provide fiber-optic broadband access in rural areas with EU funding.
72% of Lithuanian households had internet access in 2017, projected to increase to 77% by 2021.
Smartphone ownership in Lithuania was nearly 50% in 2016, expected to reach 65% by 2022.
Lithuania has the highest FTTH penetration rate in Europe as of September 2016 according to FTTH Council Europe.
Lithuania's first railway connection was established in the middle of the 19th century.
The railway network in Lithuania consists of 1,762 km of 1,520 mm gauge railway, with 122 km electrified.
The Lithuanian railway network is incompatible with European standards and requires train switching.
More than half of all inland freight in Lithuania is transported by rail.
The Trans-European standard gauge railway to Berlin is under construction.
In 2017, the Lithuanian railway operator received a penalty from the EU for breaching antitrust laws.
Transportation is the third largest sector in the Lithuanian economy.
Record-breaking orders of trucks were made by Lithuanian transport companies in 2016 and 2017.
Almost 90% of commercial truck traffic in Lithuania is international, the highest in the EU.
Lithuania has an extensive network of motorways.
The only commercial cargo port in Lithuania is handling significant cargo volumes, ranking eighth in the region.
As of 2022, the LIWA is developing a strategy to resurrect cargo shipping on the Nemunas river.
The project will involve electric ships traveling between Klaipeda and an industrial center, with an estimated investment needed.
Vilnius Airport is the largest airport in Lithuania and the 91st busiest airport in Europe, serving millions of passengers in 2016.
Other notable international airports in Lithuania include unspecified locations.
A small commercial cargo airport started regular operations in 2011, while the inland river cargo port in Marvelė began receiving cargo in 2019.
Lithuania's key energy strategy revolves around systematic diversification of energy imports and resources, defined in the National Energy Independence strategy of 2012 by Lietuvos Seimas.
Strategic energy independence initiatives in Lithuania are estimated to cost several billion euros and provide significant annual savings.

Lithuania transitioned from being an electricity exporter to an importer after decommissioning two nuclear units, the first closing in December 2004 and the second in 2009 as part of EU accession conditions.
There have been proposals for a new nuclear power plant in Lithuania, but a 2012 referendum showed 63% opposition to the project.
The main source of electrical power in Lithuania is unspecified, with other significant sources also mentioned.
Lithuania is the only Baltic state with a power plant used for power system regulation, possessing a generating capacity of 900 MW for at least 12 hours.
By 2015, Lithuania was importing 66% of its electrical power, and the first geothermal heating plant in the Baltic region was built in 2004.
Lithuania launched electricity interconnections with Sweden and Poland at the end of 2015.
In 2018, Lithuania began synchronizing its electricity grid with the rest of Europe, and in 2016, renewable sources accounted for a portion of its electricity consumption.
To combat a monopoly in the natural gas market, Lithuania built the Klaipėda LNG import terminal, named Independence, in 2014.
The Klaipėda LNG terminal can meet 100% of Lithuania's demand and 90% of Latvia's and Estonia's in the future, with a Norwegian company supplying gas until 2020.
The Gas Interconnection Poland-Lithuania (GIPL), operational since 2022, serves as a natural gas pipeline between Lithuania and Poland.
Lithuania's demographics have remained fairly homogenous since the Neolithic period.
The genetic composition of present-day Lithuanians is similar to their ancestors but not isolated from them.
Lithuanians are genetically close to Northern and Eastern European populations.

The 2021 age structure of Lithuania's population includes: 0-14 years (male: 214,113, female: 203,117), 15-64 years (male: 896,400, female: 934,467), 65 years and over (male: 195,269, female: 365,014).
The median age in Lithuania in 2022 was 44 years (41 for males, 47 for females).
The Total Fertility Rate (TFR) in Lithuania in 2021 was below replacement level.
The average age of women at childbirth was a significant demographic indicator.
As of 2021, a disproportionate number of males to females exists in the age categories 15-44.
The mean age at first childbirth for women has increased.
A notable percentage of births in 2021 were to unmarried women.
Lithuania has the most homogeneous population in the Baltic States.
Ethnic Lithuanians constitute about five-sixths of Lithuania's population.
In 2024, the population of Lithuania was approximately 2,809,977, mostly speaking Lithuanian, the official language.
The largest minority in Lithuania is concentrated in southeast Lithuania.
Tatars and Karaites are the second largest minorities in Lithuania.
As of 2021, about 2,250 Tatars and 196 Karaites live in Lithuania, mostly in Vilnius.
The official language of Lithuania is Lithuanian, but there are significant minority languages.
Minority languages with significant presence include Polish, Russian, and others.
Šalčininkai, Visaginas, and Vilnius District are areas with the greatest presence of minorities.
In 1941, Lithuania's Jewish population peaked at around 250,000, making up about 10% of the total population.
State laws guarantee education in minority languages with publicly funded schools in areas populated by minorities.
According to surveys, a large portion of the population speaks Lithuanian as their native language, with a significant number of native Russian and Polish speakers.
As of 2021, many residents speak foreign languages, including Russian, English, German, and French.
Urbanization has been steady since the 1990s, with about two-thirds of the population living in urban areas by the early 21st century.
Major cities in Lithuania include Vilnius, Kaunas, and Klaipėda.
Vilnius is highly ranked in various European city rankings based on financial and infrastructural analysis.
Lithuania has significant cities with populations listed for 2025.
Lithuania provides free state-funded healthcare to all citizens and registered long-term residents.
There is also a significant private healthcare sector in Lithuania.
From 2003 to 2012, the healthcare network of hospitals was restructured as part of healthcare reforms.
Lithuania ranked 27th in Europe in a healthcare system ranking based on waiting times and results in 2016.
In 2024, Lithuania ranked 19th in the same healthcare system ranking.
As of 2023, Lithuanian life expectancy at birth is measured for males and females, with a specified birth rate per 1,000 births.
Lithuania experienced dramatic increases in suicide rates in the 1990s, which have since decreased but remain the highest in the EU and among the highest in the world.
As of 2019, the suicide rate in Lithuania was specified per 100,000 people.
The reasons attributed to the high suicide rates include psychological issues, economic problems, social transformations, alcoholism, and bullying.
By 2000, most Lithuanian healthcare institutions were non-profit, with a developing private sector for outpatient services.
The Ministry of Health in Lithuania runs some healthcare facilities and oversees two major teaching hospitals.
The Ministry of Health is responsible for the public health network, which includes ten county public health centres and local branches.
Lithuania has 5 Territorial Health Insurance Funds covering major cities: Vilnius, Kaunas, Klaipėda, Šiauliai, and Panevėžys.
Contributions for economically active individuals in Lithuania are 9% of income.
Emergency medical services in Lithuania are provided free of charge to all residents.
Access to secondary and tertiary care in Lithuania typically requires a referral.
Lithuania has one of the lowest healthcare prices in Europe.
According to the 2021 census, the majority of residents in Lithuania are Catholics.
Catholicism has been the main religion in Lithuania since it became official in 1387.
The Catholic Church faced persecution by the Russian Empire and the Soviet Union.
Some priests led resistance against the Communist regime during the Soviet era.
A significant portion of the population is Orthodox, primarily among the Russian minority in Lithuania.
The historical presence of Lutherans in Lithuania before World War II comprised a notable part of the population.
Protestantism is represented by ethnic Lithuanians, particularly in northern and western regions and urban areas.
Newly arriving Protestant groups have established missions in Lithuania since 1990.
Hinduism is a relatively new and minority religion in Lithuania, spread by various Hindu organizations.
The International Society for Krishna Consciousness is the largest Hindu movement in Lithuania, established in 1979, with centers in major cities.
Lithuania historically had a significant Jewish community, a center of Jewish scholarship before World War II.
Almost the entire Jewish population of Lithuania (approximately 220,000) was killed during the Holocaust.
By the end of 2009, the Jewish community in Lithuania numbered around 4,000.
Romuva is a pagan faith that revives living pagan traditions, which survived in folklore and customs.
The number of people practicing Baltic faith in Lithuania increased from 1,270 to 5,118 between the years not specified and 2011.
Lithuania's education system mandates ten years of education ending at age 16 and guarantees free public higher education for qualified students.
The Ministry of Education and Science in Lithuania proposes national educational policies that are voted on in the Seimas.
In 2016, a specified percentage of GDP was allocated for education in Lithuania.
The literacy rate among Lithuanians aged 15 and older is reported to be 100%.
School attendance rates in Lithuania are above the EU average, and school dropout rates are low compared to the EU.
According to OECD data, Lithuania ranks among the top 5 countries globally for postsecondary education attainment.
In 2016, a certain percentage of the population aged 25 to 34 and another percentage aged 55 to 64 had completed tertiary education in Lithuania.
The share of tertiary-educated individuals in STEM fields in Lithuania exceeds the OECD average.
The Lithuanian education system faces multiple structural problems including insufficient funding and quality issues.
Teacher salaries in Lithuania are below the EU average, leading to national teacher strikes in 2014, 2015, and 2016.
The 2022 PISA report indicated that Lithuania's results in math, science, and reading were around the OECD average, after previously trailing behind in 2010 and 2015.
Between 2005 and 2015, the population aged 6 to 19 in Lithuania decreased by 36%, impacting the education system.
Decreasing student populations are leading to reorganizations and consolidations of schools, especially in rural areas.
There is a high volume of higher education graduates in Lithuania, with a high rate of spoken second languages contributing to the education landscape.
As of 2008, Lithuania had 15 public and 6 private universities, along with 16 public and 11 private colleges.
One university mentioned is the largest in Lithuania.
Another university mentioned is the largest technical university in the Baltic States and the second largest university in Lithuania.
To adapt to a decreasing number of high-school students, the Lithuanian parliament decided to reduce the number of universities in Lithuania.
In early 2018, two universities were merged.
The Lithuanian language (lietuvių kalba) is the official state language of Lithuania.
There are about 3 million native Lithuanian speakers in Lithuania and approximately 0.5 million abroad.
Lithuanian is a Baltic language, closely related to Latvian, but they are not mutually intelligible.
Lithuanian is written in an adapted version of the Latin alphabet.
Lithuanian is considered a 'Conservative' living language, retaining many features of Proto-Indo-European.
The language studies are important for linguistics and the reconstruction of historical languages.
The two main dialects of the Lithuanian language are Aukštaitian and Samogitian.
Aukštaitian is used mainly in central, southern, and eastern Lithuania, while Samogitian is used in the western part and is sometimes considered a separate language.
The main distinction between the two dialects is the pronunciation of accented and unaccented vowels.
The groundwork for written Lithuanian was laid by noblemen and scholars in the 16th and 17th centuries, with contributions to dictionaries and publications.
The first grammar book of the Lithuanian language, *Grammatica Litvanica*, published in 1653 by Daniel Klein.
Daniel Klein's work was significant for the transition from dialects to a standard Lithuanian language.
Klein introduced the letter *ū* into Lithuanian writing.
There is a significant body of Lithuanian literature written in Latin, the main scholarly language of the Middle Ages.
The edicts of the Lithuanian King are a prime example of early Lithuanian Latin literature.
One of the first Lithuanian authors to write in Latin was an unnamed poet active around 1480 – after 1533.
The poem *Carmen de statura, feritate ac venatione bisontis* describes the Lithuanian landscape, life, and the clash of paganism and Christianity.
An individual under the pseudonym (name not provided) wrote *On the Customs of Tatars, Lithuanians and Muscovites*, published in 1615 but written in the mid-16th century.
Petrus Roysius Maurus Alcagnicensis was an influential lawyer and poet in 16th-century Lithuanian cultural life.
The publicist (name not provided) wrote a history of Lithuania in Latin around 1560.
Loannes Radvanus wrote an epic poem, *Radivilias*, aimed to be the Lithuanian national epic, published in 1588.
17th-century Lithuanian scholars continued to write in Latin in fields such as theology, rhetoric, and music.
(Unnamed author) wrote the first printed Lithuanian history, *Historia Lithuania*.
Lithuanian literary works began being published in the 16th century.
The first printed Lithuanian book was *Katekizmo prasti žodžiai* (*The Simple Words of Catechism*), published in 1547.
The early Lithuanian literature was primarily religious in nature during the 16th and 17th centuries.
The evolution of old Lithuanian literature (14th–18th century) concludes with poet Donelaitis and his poem *Metai* (*The Seasons*).
Lithuanian literature in the first half of the 19th century combined sentimentalism and realism.
The Tsarist annexation in the 19th century led to the Book Smugglers movement, essential for the survival of the Lithuanian language and literature.
20th-century Lithuanian literature featured prominent authors and works that contributed to the literary landscape.
In the 21st century, new authors like Renata Šerelytė and Valdas Papievis debuted in Lithuanian literature.
Lithuania produced notable architects who introduced various architectural movements, particularly between the 17th and 19th centuries.
Vilnius is recognized as a capital of Eastern European Baroque architecture, known for its Baroque churches and buildings.
Lithuania is home to about twenty castles, with many reconstructed or surviving partially.
The preservation of Lithuanian village life and historic architecture is evident, with open-air museums showcasing traditional ethnographic architecture.
Architectural styles like Lithuanian National Romanticism emerged in Lithuania's temporary capital during the interwar period, showcasing Art Deco influences.
The largest museum of art conservation and display in Lithuania was founded in 1933.
The National Gallery of Art presents a collection of Lithuanian art from the 20th and 21st centuries.
Two private museums were opened in 2018 dedicated to modern Lithuanian art and art heritage.
The composer M.K. Čiurlionis (1875–1911) is a renowned figure in Lithuania's art community.
An asteroid honors M.K. Čiurlionis for his achievements.
Kaunas houses the only military museum in Lithuania.
Prominent Lithuanian painters of the 18th and 19th centuries include various unnamed artists.
Lithuania has theaters located in major cities including Vilnius and Klaipėda.
Key theater festivals in Lithuania include Sirenos and TheATRIUM.
Notable Lithuanian theatre directors include Oskaras Koršunovas and Cezaris Graužinis.
The first Lithuanian cinema sessions started on 28 July 1896.
Pioneers of Lithuanian cinema released early films in 1909.
Pranas Valuskis filmed *Naktis Lietuvoje* in 1925, marking an early contribution to Lithuanian film.
The oldest operational cinema in Lithuania was opened in 1940.
Despite challenges during occupation, directors created valuable films post-independence.
In 2018, 4,265,414 cinema tickets were sold in Lithuania.
Lithuanian folk music is connected with Lithuanian culture and consists of two instrument cultures: stringed and wind.
Lithuanian folk music is archaic and mostly used for ritual purposes, containing elements of faith.
There are three ancient styles of singing in Lithuania connected with ethnographical regions.
Folk song genres in Lithuania include Sutartinės (Multipart Songs), Wedding Songs, War-Historical Time Songs, Calendar Cycle and Ritual Songs, and Work Songs.
The first opera organized by Italian artists in Lithuania took place on 4 September 1636 at the order of a notable figure.
Operas are currently staged in Lithuania by various independent troupes.
Mikalojus Konstantinas Čiurlionis was a Lithuanian painter and composer known for influencing modern Lithuanian culture.
Čiurlionis created about 200 pieces of music and contributed to symbolism and art nouveau, becoming a pioneer of abstract art in Europe.
Vilnius is the only city in Lithuania with three choir laureates at the European Grand Prix for Choral Singing.
The *Dainų šventė* (Song Festival) is a prominent event in Lithuania that gathers thousands of singers and dancers every four years since its inception in 1924.
The Lithuanian Song and Dance Festival was inscribed as UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage.
*Gatvės muzikos diena* (Street Music Day) is an annual event that gathers musicians of various genres.
Modern classical composers in Lithuania emerged in the seventies, exploring archaic Lithuanian music with modern styles.
The jazz scene in Lithuania was active during Soviet occupation.
In 1970-71, the Ganelin/Tarasov/Chekasin trio established the Vilnius Jazz School.
Birštonas Jazz is one of the most known annual jazz events in Lithuania.
The MICL organization collects, promotes, and shares information about Lithuanian musical culture.
After the Soviet occupation of the Baltic states in 1944, strict control was exercised over artistic expressions in Lithuania.
First local rock bands in Lithuania emerged around 1965, including Kertukai, Aitvarai, and Nuogi ant slenksčio.
Lithuanian artists organized patriotic events and used metaphors in their music to express dissent.
The band Antis mocked the Soviet regime through their metaphorical lyrics.
The song 'Zombiai' by Antis indirectly referenced Soviet soldiers occupying Lithuania.
The song 'Kolorado vabalai' was a favorite for its hidden criticism of the Soviets.
The rock band Foje gained popularity in the early years of Lithuanian independence, attracting large audiences.
After Foje disbanded in 1997, its vocalist remained an influential figure in Lithuanian music and charity events.
Foje is known for the unofficial Lithuanian sports anthem 'Trys milijonai' and the official anthem 'Nebetyli sirgaliai.'
Lithuanian cuisine features locally grown products suitable for the country's cool climate.
Dairy products are a specialty of Lithuanian cuisine, and coastal region dishes are very popular.
Lithuanian cuisine shares similarities with Northern European cuisine but retains unique characteristics due to the country's history.
Dairy products are significant in traditional Lithuanian cuisine, including white cottage cheese, curd, soured milk, sour cream, butter, and sour cream butter.
Traditional meat products in Lithuania are typically seasoned, matured, and smoked items such as smoked sausages, lard, and smoked ham.
Various soups are integral to Lithuanian tradition and diet, including boletus soup, cabbage soup, beer soup, milk soup, cold-beet soup, and assorted porridges.
The consumption of freshwater fish, herring, wild berries, mushrooms, and honey remains popular in Lithuania.
Rye bread is one of the oldest and most fundamental Lithuanian foods, consumed daily and significant in family rituals and agrarian ceremonies.
Lithuanian cuisine is influenced by other nations, introducing dishes like potato pudding and Kishka, along with baroque tree cakes.
Eastern (Karaite) cuisine has added variety to Lithuanian food, with specific popular dishes.
Beer is the most common alcoholic beverage in Lithuania, with a brewing tradition dating back to ancient times, mentioned in chronicles since the 11th century.
Lithuania's farmhouse brewing traditions led to a commercial brewing culture, with Lithuania ranking in the top 5 in Europe for beer consumption per capita in 2015.
Lithuania has 75 active breweries, including 32 microbreweries, with a growing focus on microbrews in bars across the country.
Eight Lithuanian restaurants are listed in the White Guide Baltic Top 30.
The local '30 geriausių restoranų' guide lists top domestic restaurants.
Lithuanian restaurants will appear on 13 June 2024 in the local guide.
The government generally respects freedom of expression rights in practice.
An independent press, effective judiciary, and functioning democratic political system promote freedoms in Lithuania.
Certain acts, such as incitement to hatred, violence, and disinformation, are not protected under freedom of expression.
It is a crime to deny or trivialize Soviet or Nazi German crimes against Lithuania.
In 2021, the best-selling daily national newspapers were identified along with their circulating numbers.
LNK, BTV, and TV6 were the most popular television networks in Lithuania in 2021.
The most popular radio stations in Lithuania in 2021 were M-1 and Lietus.
Lithuania celebrates Statehood Day on 6 July, commemorating the establishment by Mindaugas in 1253.
February 16 is celebrated as the day of independence declaration from Russia and Germany in 1918.
Lithuania has 13 public holidays as of 2018, which come with a day off.
An annual event commemorates the anniversary of a notable figure's death and attracts thousands of visitors and craftsmen.
Key Lithuanian festivals are celebrated throughout the year, each with specific historical and cultural significance.
January 1 marks the New Year's Day in Lithuania.
February 16 celebrates the State Restoration Day in Lithuania.
March 11 is observed as the Day of Restoration of Independence in Lithuania.
Easter (Velykos) commemorates the resurrection of Jesus, falling on the first Sunday after the first full moon post March 21.
May 1 is designated as International Workers' Day.
The first Sunday in May is celebrated as Mother's Day.
The first Sunday in June is Father's Day.
June 24 celebrates the Day of Dew (Rasos) based on pagan traditions.
July 6 is Statehood Day in Lithuania, commemorating the coronation of the first King Mindaugas in 1253.
August 15 is celebrated as Assumption Day, also linked to pagan traditions.
November 1 is All Saints' Day, which is gaining popularity and is informally celebrated on Halloween eve.
November 2 memorializes the Day of the Dead.
December 24 is Christmas Eve (Kūčios).
December 25 and 26 are celebrated as Christmas (Kalėdos), commemorating the birth of Jesus.
Basketball is the most popular sport in Lithuania, with the national team having won multiple Eurobasket medals.
76% of Lithuania's population watched men's national basketball games live in 2014.
Lithuania hosted the Eurobasket in 1939 and other occasions.
The historic basketball team of Lithuania from Kaunas won the European basketball league in 1999.
Lithuania has a notable number of basketball players, including Hall of Fame inductees and current NBA players.
Lithuania has won a total of Olympic medals, including 6 gold medals in swimming.
Other Lithuanians have won Olympic medals representing the Soviet Union.
The most successful Olympic athlete of independent Lithuania has won gold medals in two Olympic games and a bronze medal, along with numerous other medals.
A 15-year-old swimmer from Lithuania recently won a gold medal at the Olympics in London, which increased the sport's popularity in Lithuania.
Lithuania hosted a FIFA tournament for the first time.
Few Lithuanian athletes have found success in winter sports, despite the presence of facilities like ice rinks and skiing slopes, including the first indoor ski slope in the Baltic states.
In 2018, Lithuanian athletes won gold medals at the European Championships.
Graduate School of International Relations and Pacific Studies is part of the University of California, San Diego.
A pattern of compatible majorities and cohabitation emerged in Lithuania, similar to the French case.
The ability of the Lithuanian president to influence government formation declined after losing a sympathetic parliamentary majority.
